title: US and Iran Agree Peace Deal; Strait of Hormuz to Reopen as Oil Falls
content: On 14 June 2026, the US and Iran announced a deal to end their nearly four-month war, with Pakistan mediating and a formal signing scheduled in Geneva. Key terms include Iran resuming oil exports, the US naval blockade lifting, and the Strait of Hormuz reopening under a 60-day ceasefire framework allowing nuclear talks. Brent crude fell more than $4/barrel on the announcement, unwinding part of the Iran-war energy premium that had driven global inflation sharply higher since February. Equity futures surged on 16 June. Planned technical talks in Switzerland faced postponement on 19 June, adding near-term uncertainty, but the deal markedly reduces the tail risk that had reshaped central bank policy globally.

title: Fed Holds Rates but Dot Plot Flips Hawkish; Warsh Era Signals Rate Hike Ahead
content: The FOMC voted 12-0 on 17 June 2026 to hold the federal funds rate at 3.50%–3.75% at Kevin Warsh's inaugural meeting as Fed Chair, but the updated dot plot delivered a hawkish jolt. Nine of 18 policymakers now project at least one 25bp rate hike before year-end 2026, with the median dot implying a year-end rate of 3.8%—erasing earlier cut signals. The Fed removed forward guidance pointing to future reductions. Treasury markets reacted sharply: the 2-year UST yield jumped ~14bp to 4.19%, with money markets fully pricing a hike by October. The S&P 500 fell ~1.2% on 18 June and the USD strengthened broadly, reflecting a decisive shift to 'higher for longer'.

title: Bank of Japan Raises Policy Rate to 1%, Highest Level Since 1995
content: The Bank of Japan's Policy Board voted 7-1 on 16 June 2026 to raise its policy rate by 25 basis points to 1.00%—the highest since September 1995—continuing its gradual monetary normalisation. The sole dissenter, board member Asada Toichiro, cited downside risks to output and employment. The BoJ cited Iran-war-driven energy costs as a risk to underlying inflation exceeding the 2% target and reaffirmed its plan to taper JGB purchases by ¥200bn per quarter, targeting monthly purchases of ¥2tn from April 2027. The decision was broadly in line with market expectations but meaningfully narrowed the US–Japan yield differential, supporting JPY and pressuring carry-trade positions.
